file_name: Yu et al. - 2025 - An Automated and Interpretable Computer-Aided Approach for Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using Genetic Progr.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Genetic Programming (GP), an evolutionary computation/AI method, to automatically learn features and classify skin cancer images. It proposes a computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) system for skin cancer diagnosis using GP to evolve variable-length models for feature extraction and classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a new computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) system based on genetic programming to automatically learn features for skin cancer classification. It presents original experimental results, including comparisons with GP-based, neural network-based, and traditional methods on five real-world skin cancer classification tasks, and analyzes the effectiveness of a duplicate subtree removing mechanism. The structure includes an abstract, introduction, methodology, experimental results, and conclusion, which are characteristic of an original research article rather than a review.

title: An Automated and Interpretable Computer-Aided Approach for Skin Cancer Diagnosis Using Genetic Programming
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated and interpretable computer-aided diagnosis system for skin cancer using genetic programming to automatically learn effective features. The approach employs a novel duplicate subtree removing mechanism to simplify models and enhance interpretability while evolving variable-length feature extraction models. The method was evaluated on five real-world skin cancer classification tasks derived from the PH2, MEDNODE, and HAM10000 datasets. Performance was assessed using average balanced accuracy via five-fold cross-validation on the training set and testing on unseen data. Results indicate that the proposed approach achieves better performance than GP-based, neural network-based, and traditional comparison methods in most cases. The study concludes that the system effectively reduces model complexity while maintaining high diagnostic accuracy and interpretability.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in PH2binary, PH2multi, MEDNODE, HAMbinary, and HAMmulti tasks. In PH2binary, it is the malignant class. In MEDNODE, it is one of two classes (70 images). In HAMbinary, it is the malignant class (80 images). In HAMmulti, it is one of seven classes from HAM10000.

Studied in PH2binary (common and atypical nevi grouped as benign class, 160 images total), PH2multi (80 common, 80 atypical), MEDNODE (benign tumors, 100 images), HAMbinary (120 NV images as benign class), and HAMmulti (NV class from HAM10000).
Included in the HAM10000 dataset which was used for the HAMmulti task. Specific count not reported for the subset used, but listed as one of the seven classes.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant, or specific lesion classes such as melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on skin cancer image classification tasks. It evaluates the proposed Genetic Programming approach on five real-world skin cancer classification tasks: PH2binary (binary: melanoma vs. benign), PH2multi (multiclass: melanoma, common nevus, atypical nevus), MEDNODE (binary: melanoma vs. benign tumors), HAMbinary (binary: melanoma vs. benign), and HAMmulti (multiclass: seven classes from HAM10000). The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels using classifiers like SVM, NB, RF, and KNN on evolved features. Performance is measured by average balanced accuracy.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database consisting of 200 images taken by professional dermatoscopy with an image size of approximately 768 × 560. It contains three types of images: melanomas, common nevi, and atypical nevi.

use.training: Used for PH2binary and PH2multi classification tasks. Divided into 1:1 ratio of train set and test set. Five-fold cross-validation performed on the training set for fitness evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing in PH2binary and PH2multi tasks. The test set remains unseen during the evolutionary process.

datasets_used.name: MEDNODE
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 170 skin lesion images captured by digital cameras (non-dermoscopic), with image sizes ranging from 257 × 201 to 1333 × 9531.

use.training: Used for binary classification task. Divided into 1:1 ratio of train set and test set. Five-fold cross-validation performed on the training set for fitness evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing in the MEDNODE binary classification task. The test set remains unseen during the evolutionary process.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The original dataset contains 10,015 dermoscopic images with an image size of 450 × 600. It has a high degree of class imbalance, with the NV class accounting for two-thirds of the total.

use.training: Used to derive HAMbinary and HAMmulti tasks. For HAMbinary, 120 NV images and 80 MEL images were used. For HAMmulti, the data size was reduced to one-tenth of the original while maintaining class proportions. All tasks divided into 1:1 ratio of train set and test set. Five-fold cross-validation performed on the training set for fitness evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing in HAMbinary and HAMmulti tasks. The test set remains unseen during the evolutionary process.

datasets_used.name: HAMbinary
datasets_used.description: A binary classification task derived from the HAM10000 dataset to simulate real-world scenarios with less data. It contains 120 NV images as benign classes and 80 MEL images as malignant classes.

use.training: Used for binary classification. Divided into 1:1 ratio of train set and test set. Five-fold cross-validation performed on the training set for fitness evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing in the HAMbinary task. The test set remains unseen during the evolutionary process.

datasets_used.name: HAMmulti
datasets_used.description: A multiclass classification task derived from the HAM10000 dataset. It maintains the class proportions of the original HAM10000 but reduces the data size to one-tenth for multiclass tasks.

use.training: Used for multiclass classification. Divided into 1:1 ratio of train set and test set. Five-fold cross-validation performed on the training set for fitness evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing in the HAMmulti task. The test set remains unseen during the evolutionary process.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To show the significance of improvement in classification accuracy between the proposed NDSGP approach and comparison methods (GP-based, traditional, and CNN-based).
Used as the significance threshold for the Wilcoxon rank-sum test.
Used for fitness evaluation during the evolutionary process on the training set.
To report the classification accuracy and number of features used over 30 runs of the experiments.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in classification accuracy between the proposed NDSGP approach and the comparison methods.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in classification accuracy between the proposed NDSGP approach and the comparison methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Wilcoxon rank-sum test with a 95% confidence level was used to show the significance of improvement. The symbols '+', '=', and '-' in the tables indicate that the performance of NDSGP is significantly better, similar, and significantly worse than the compared method, respectively.

NDSGP.description: Outstanding performance, significantly better than traditional methods in most cases. Specifically, significantly better than Histogram in 12 cases and Color Variation in 12 cases. Achieved best performance among all compared methods.

Traditional Methods (Geometry, uLBP, GLC.description: NDSGP achieved significantly better performance than Geometry, GLCM, and HOG in 19 cases. Significantly better than uLBP in 15 cases. Histogram performed well on this simple problem but failed on complex ones.

CNN-based Methods.description: NDSGP achieved better average accuracy than CNN-based methods.

PH2binary.notes: Binary classification of melanoma vs. benign (common + atypical nevi). Images resized to 383x287. Divided into 1:1 train/test set.

NDSGP.description: Outstanding performance. Significantly better than traditional methods in most cases. Failed to solve as well as on PH2binary compared to Histogram, indicating complexity.

Traditional Methods.description: NDSGP significantly outperformed traditional methods. Histogram performed poorly on this complex problem.

CNN-based Methods.description: NDSGP achieved better average accuracy than CNN-based methods.

PH2multi.notes: Multiclass classification of melanoma, common nevus, and atypical nevus. Images resized to 383x287. Divided into 1:1 train/test set.

NDSGP (with RF).description: Highest average accuracy among all methods on this dataset.

CNN-based Methods.description: Performed worse than NDSGP (with RF and SVM).

MEDNODE.notes: Binary classification of melanoma vs. benign tumors. Non-dermoscopic images. NDSGP using RF and SVM performed better than CNN-based methods. This is the only dataset where NDSGP did not achieve better average accuracy than CNN-based methods across all classifiers, but specific RF/SVM variants did.

NDSGP.description: Outstanding performance. Significantly better than traditional methods in most cases. Significantly better than Histogram in 12 cases and Color Variation in 12 cases. Better average accuracy than CNN-based methods.

Traditional Methods.description: NDSGP significantly outperformed Geometry, GLCM, and HOG in 19 cases. Significantly better than uLBP in 15 cases.

CNN-based Methods.description: NDSGP achieved better average accuracy than CNN-based methods.

HAMbinary.notes: Binary classification derived from HAM10000. 120 NV (benign) and 80 MEL (malignant) images. Simulates real-world scenario with less data. Images resized to 383x287. Divided into 1:1 train/test set.

NDSGP.description: Outstanding performance. Significantly better than traditional methods in most cases. Better average accuracy than CNN-based methods.

CNN-based Methods.description: NDSGP achieved better average accuracy than CNN-based methods.

HAMmulti.notes: Multiclass classification derived from HAM10000. Maintains class proportions of original HAM10000 but reduces data size to one-tenth. Seven classes: MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC. Images resized to 383x287. Divided into 1:1 train/test set.

architecture_used.main_model: NDSGP (New Duplicate Subtree Removing Genetic Programming)
architecture_used.input_size: 383 x 287 pixels (RGB images resized to this dimension)

architecture_details.type: Genetic Programming (GP) based feature extraction and classification system
architecture_details.program_structure: Variable-length tree structure with input, feature extraction, feature concatenation, and output layers
architecture_details.mechanism: Duplicate subtree removing mechanism to simplify models and enhance interpretability
classification_methods: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with linear kernel
Random Forest (RF) with 100 trees and max depth 10

evolutionary_parameters.cache_table_size: 5 x N (where N is population size)

classification_or_output_head.type: External classifiers applied to evolved features
classification_or_output_head.details: Features extracted by the GP tree are concatenated and fed into SVM, KNN, RF, or NB classifiers. For CNN comparisons, LeNet-5 (3 conv layers, max pooling, flattening, 2 dense layers, ReLU, softmax) and CNN-8 (2 conv layers with max pooling/dropout, flattening, dense layers, ReLU, softmax) were used.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: NDSGP (New Duplicate Subtree Removing Genetic Programming)
preprocessing_techniques: Artifact removal using black hat operation and image inpainting to eliminate gel, reflections, and hair, resulting in a noiseless RGB image (NRGB)
Grayscale transformation of the NRGB image
Thresholding to obtain a black-and-white binary image
Morphological opening and dilation operations on the binary image for noise removal
Lesion region segmentation via maximum contour drawing to generate a lesion mask (Mask)
Color channel separation (Red, Green, Blue) from the NRGB image
conclusions: The proposed NDSGP approach, which uses genetic programming to automatically learn features, achieves better performance than GP-based, neural network-based, and traditional comparison methods in most cases across five real-world skin cancer classification tasks.

NDSGP significantly outperforms GP-based methods (FGP and FLGP), achieving classification accuracy nearly 10% higher than FLGP and almost 8% higher than FGP on the PH2 dataset, and nearly 4% higher on MEDNODE.

NDSGP demonstrates outstanding performance on binary and multiclass tasks (PH2binary, PH2multi, HAMbinary, HAMmulti), achieving significantly better results than traditional feature extraction methods (Geometry, uLBP, GLCM, HOG, Histogram, Color Variation, HSV) in most cases.

NDSGP is more effective than CNN-based methods (LeNet-5 and CNN-8) on datasets with limited samples and class imbalance, achieving the highest average accuracy of 74.77% on the MEDNODE dataset using Random Forest.

The duplicate subtree removing mechanism effectively simplifies the model by reducing the number of features used (often by an order of magnitude compared to FGP) and enhancing stability, although it results in a slight sacrifice in classification accuracy compared to the non-duplicate version.
NDSGP produces models with potentially high interpretability and smaller tree structures compared to other GP methods, facilitating the visualization and understanding of evolved solutions.
Traditional methods like Histogram perform well on simple problems (e.g., PH2binary) but fail on complex problems (e.g., PH2multi), whereas NDSGP handles varying difficulty levels more robustly.

The study confirms that automated feature learning via GP can serve as a viable and interpretable alternative to manual feature engineering and deep learning for skin cancer diagnosis, particularly when domain knowledge for feature extraction is lacking or network design is complex.
important_remarks: The study relies on five-fold cross-validation performed exclusively on the training set for fitness evaluation, with a separate, unseen test set used for final performance assessment; this mitigates data leakage but limits the scope of validation to internal splits.

The HAM10000 dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with the melanocytic nevus (NV) class accounting for two-thirds of the total images; the HAMmulti task uses a reduced subset (one-tenth of original) maintaining these proportions, which may bias results toward the majority class.

The MEDNODE dataset consists of non-dermoscopic images captured by digital cameras with highly variable image sizes (257 × 201 to 1333 × 9531), introducing complexity and potential bias compared to the standardized dermoscopic images in PH2 and HAM10000.

All classification tasks were artificially balanced by dividing the data into a 1:1 ratio of training and test sets, which does not reflect the real-world prevalence of skin cancer classes and may overestimate clinical utility.

The proposed NDSGP approach was evaluated on small sample sizes (e.g., PH2 has only 200 images, HAMbinary has 200 images), limiting the generalizability of the findings to larger, more diverse clinical populations.
The method requires resizing all images to 383 × 287 pixels, which may distort aspect ratios and lose spatial information, particularly for the non-uniformly sized MEDNODE images.

While the paper claims high interpretability due to the duplicate subtree removing mechanism, it does not provide clinical validation of this interpretability by dermatologists, relying instead on structural metrics like tree size.

The comparison with CNN-based methods (LeNet-5 and CNN-8) uses relatively simple architectures trained for only 50 epochs, which may underestimate the potential performance of modern deep learning models on these tasks.

The study focuses on binary and multiclass classification accuracy but does not report sensitivity, specificity, or AUC-ROC metrics, which are critical for evaluating diagnostic test performance in clinical settings.

The preprocessing pipeline includes artifact removal (gel, reflections, hair) and segmentation, which assumes high-quality input images and may fail in noisy clinical environments, limiting deployment robustness.
